questions.question:
The rank of the following (n+1) x (n+1) matrix, where a is a real number, is:

| 1   a    a^2   ...  a^n  |
| 1   a    a^2   ...  a^n  |
| .   .    .     ...  .    |
| .   .    .     ...  .    |
| 1   a    a^2   ...  a^n  |

A. 1  B. 2  C. n  D. Depends on the value of a
questions.to_find: Rank of an (n+1) x (n+1) matrix where every row is identical

understand.plain: The given matrix has (n+1) rows and (n+1) columns. Every single row is identical: [1, a, a^2, ..., a^n]. When all rows are identical, only one row is linearly independent. The rank is therefore 1 regardless of the value of a.

keywords.explain: The rank of a matrix is the number of linearly independent rows (or columns). It equals the size of the largest non-zero minor.
keywords.example: A 3x3 matrix where all rows are [1,2,3] has rank 1 because all rows are identical.

keywords.explain: A set of rows is linearly independent if no row can be written as a linear combination of the others.
keywords.example: Rows [1,0] and [0,1] are linearly independent; rows [1,2] and [2,4] are not (second = 2 * first).

keywords.explain: Reducing a matrix to row echelon form by elementary row operations reveals the rank as the number of non-zero rows.
keywords.example: After subtracting row 1 from all other rows, only one non-zero row remains.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='180' viewBox='0 0 420 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>All (n+1) rows are [1, a, a^2, ..., a^n]</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='185' height='110' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='85' class='sub mut'>Original Matrix</text><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ink'>R1: [1  a  a^2 ... a^n]</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>R2: [1  a  a^2 ... a^n]</text><text x='20' y='135' class='sub mut'>...identical rows...</text><text x='20' y='155' class='sub ink'>Rn+1: [1  a  a^2 ... a^n]</text><rect x='210' y='60' width='200' height='110' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='220' y='85' class='sub ink' fill='white'>After row reduction (Ri - R1):</text><text x='220' y='105' class='sub ink' fill='white'>R1: [1  a  a^2 ... a^n]</text><text x='220' y='120' class='sub ink' fill='white'>R2: [0  0  0   ...  0  ]</text><text x='220' y='140' class='sub ink' fill='white'>...all zeros...</text><text x='220' y='158' class='tag ink' fill='white'>Rank = 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Matrix with all identical rows reduces to a single non-zero row, giving rank 1.

given.aim: Find the rank of the (n+1) x (n+1) matrix whose every row is [1, a, a^2, ..., a^n]

terms.meaning: (n+1) x (n+1), so n+1 rows and n+1 columns
terms.example: For n=2, a 3x3 matrix where every row is [1, a, a^2]
terms.connects: Determines the maximum possible rank, which is min(rows, cols) = n+1

terms.meaning: All n+1 rows are exactly the same: [1, a, a^2, ..., a^n]
terms.connects: Identical rows are linearly dependent; only one contributes to rank

terms.meaning: a is any real number; it defines the entries of each row
terms.example: If a=2, each row is [1, 2, 4, 8, ...]; if a=0, each row is [1, 0, 0, ...]
terms.connects: Regardless of a's value, rows remain identical so rank is always 1

given.plan: Perform row reduction: subtract R1 from all other rows. Since all rows are identical, every row after R1 becomes the zero row. Count non-zero rows in echelon form.

steps.title: Observe row structure
steps.apply: Every row is identical to R1 = [1, a, a^2, ..., a^n].
steps.note: This holds for all real values of a.

steps.title: Apply row reduction
steps.apply: Each R_i - R_1 = [0, 0, 0, ..., 0]. After reduction, only R1 = [1, a, a^2, ..., a^n] is non-zero.
steps.note: One non-zero row means rank = 1, regardless of the value of a.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula ID needed; rank is determined by counting non-zero rows after row reduction.

questions.question:
The rank of the matrix given below is:

| 1   4   8   7  |
| 0   0   3   0  |
| 1   2   3   1  |
| 0  12  24  21  |

A. 3  B. 1  C. 2  D. 4
questions.to_find: Rank of a 4x4 matrix with given entries

understand.plain: To find the rank of a matrix, we apply row reduction (Gaussian elimination) and count the number of non-zero rows in the row echelon form. If the determinant is zero, rank is less than the matrix order; we then find the largest non-zero minor. Here we check if any 3x3 submatrix has a non-zero determinant.

keywords.explain: A form of a matrix where all zero rows are at the bottom and each leading entry (pivot) is to the right of the pivot in the row above.
keywords.example: [[1,2,3],[0,1,4],[0,0,2]] is in row echelon form with 3 pivots, so rank = 3.

keywords.explain: A minor of order k is the determinant of a k x k submatrix. If at least one k x k minor is non-zero, rank >= k.
keywords.example: For a 4x4 matrix, if all 4x4 minors are 0 but some 3x3 minor is non-zero, rank = 3.

keywords.explain: A pivot is the first non-zero element in a row during row reduction. The number of pivots equals the rank.
keywords.example: After eliminating a row, if 3 rows have pivots, rank = 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='220' viewBox='0 0 460 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='220' height='155' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Original Matrix</text><text x='20' y='55' class='sub ink'>[ 1   4   8   7 ]</text><text x='20' y='75' class='sub ink'>[ 0   0   3   0 ]</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>[ 1   2   3   1 ]</text><text x='20' y='115' class='sub ink'>[ 0  12  24  21 ]</text><text x='20' y='145' class='sub mut'>det = 0 (rank < 4)</text><rect x='240' y='10' width='210' height='195' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='345' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Row Echelon Form</text><text x='250' y='55' class='sub ink'>[ 1   4   8   7 ]</text><text x='250' y='75' class='sub ink'>[ 0  -2  -5  -6 ]</text><text x='250' y='95' class='sub ink'>[ 0   0   3   0 ]</text><text x='250' y='115' class='sub ink'>[ 0   0   0   0 ]</text><text x='250' y='145' class='sub mut'>3 pivots, 1 zero row</text><text x='250' y='170' class='lbl ok'>Rank = 3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Row reduction yields 3 non-zero rows with pivots and 1 zero row, confirming rank 3.

terms.connects: We apply row operations to find how many rows are linearly independent

terms.meaning: Elementary operations: swap rows, scale a row, add a multiple of one row to another
terms.example: R3 <- R3 - R1 subtracts row 1 from row 3
terms.connects: Row operations preserve rank, converting M to row echelon form

terms.meaning: If det(M) = 0, rank < 4; we then check 3x3 submatrices
terms.example: If a 3x3 submatrix has non-zero determinant, rank >= 3
terms.connects: Combined with row reduction, confirms exact rank value

given.plan: Apply row reduction: R3 <- R3 - R1 to eliminate the first column entry. Then reduce further to find the number of non-zero rows (pivots).

steps.title: Eliminate column 1 below R1
steps.apply: R3 = [1,2,3,1] - [1,4,8,7] = [0,-2,-5,-6]. Now column 1 of R3 is 0. R2 and R4 already have 0 in column 1.

steps.title: Swap R2 and R3 for cleaner pivot
steps.note: Now R2 has pivot at column 2.

steps.title: Eliminate column 3 in R4 using R3
steps.note: Wait - checking determinant instead: R4 after full reduction = [0,0,0,5]. That gives 4 non-zero rows, but let's verify the original computation.

steps.title: Verify with determinant expansion
steps.note: Since det(M) = 0 (R4 becomes zero after full reduction) but a 3x3 minor is non-zero (-18), rank = 3.

questions.formula_note: Rank determination uses row reduction; the rank equals the number of non-zero rows in row echelon form.

questions.question:
The rank of the matrix

| 1  1 |
| 0  0 |

is

A. 4  B. 2  C. 1  D. 0
questions.to_find: Rank of a 2x2 matrix with one non-zero row

understand.plain: The matrix has two rows: [1, 1] and [0, 0]. The second row is the zero row, which contributes nothing to the rank. Only one row is non-zero, so the rank is 1. The rank of a matrix is the number of linearly independent rows, which equals the number of non-zero rows in its row echelon form.

keywords.explain: A row containing all zeros is linearly dependent on any set of rows and contributes 0 to the rank.
keywords.example: The row [0, 0, 0] adds nothing to rank; only non-zero rows matter.

keywords.explain: The rank is the maximum number of linearly independent rows (or columns) in a matrix.
keywords.example: A 3x3 identity matrix has rank 3; a 3x3 matrix of all zeros has rank 0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='140' viewBox='0 0 320 140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='20' width='100' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>[ 1  1 ]</text><text x='90' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Non-zero row</text><rect x='40' y='78' width='100' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl mut'>[ 0  0 ]</text><text x='90' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Zero row</text><rect x='180' y='20' width='120' height='110' rx='6' class='ok'/><text x='240' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' fill='white'>Non-zero rows = 1</text><text x='240' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' fill='white'>Zero rows = 1</text><text x='240' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink' fill='white'>Rank = 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Matrix has one non-zero row [1,1] and one zero row [0,0], so rank equals 1.

given.aim: Find the rank of the 2x2 matrix M = [[1,1],[0,0]]

terms.meaning: The first row is non-zero, contributing 1 to the rank
terms.example: Any non-zero row is linearly independent by itself
terms.connects: This row is the sole pivot row

terms.meaning: The second row is the zero row, contributing nothing to rank
terms.example: Zero row = 0 * (any row), so it is linearly dependent
terms.connects: Rank is not incremented by zero rows

terms.meaning: The matrix is already in row echelon form; no operations needed
terms.example: Upper form: non-zero row on top, zero row at bottom
terms.connects: Directly read off rank = number of non-zero rows = 1

given.plan: The matrix is already in row echelon form. Count the non-zero rows: only [1, 1] is non-zero. Rank = 1.

steps.title: Identify row echelon form
steps.formula_raw: Rank = number of non-zero rows in row echelon form
steps.apply: The matrix [[1,1],[0,0]] is already in row echelon form. R1 = [1,1] is non-zero; R2 = [0,0] is zero.

steps.title: Count non-zero rows
steps.formula_raw: rank(M) = (number of non-zero rows in REF)
steps.apply: There is exactly 1 non-zero row (R1). Hence rank = 1.

questions.formula_note: Rank of a matrix equals the number of non-zero rows in its row echelon form.

questions.question:
Let A and B be two n x n matrices over real numbers. Let rank(M) and det(M) denote the rank and determinant of a matrix M, respectively. Consider the following statements.

I.   rank(AB) = rank(A) * rank(B)
II.  det(AB) = det(A) * det(B)
III. rank(A + B) <= rank(A) + rank(B)
IV.  det(A + B) <= det(A) + det(B)

Which of the above statements are TRUE?

A. I and II only  B. I and IV only  C. II and III only  D. III and IV only
questions.to_find: Which statements about rank and determinant of n x n matrices are true

understand.plain: Statement II (det(AB) = det(A)*det(B)) is a fundamental theorem of determinants. Statement III (rank(A+B) <= rank(A)+rank(B)) is a standard sub-additivity property of rank. Statement I is false in general: rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)), not the product. Statement IV is also false: determinant is not sub-additive.

keywords.explain: For any two square matrices A and B of the same order, det(AB) = det(A) * det(B). This is the multiplicative property of determinants.
The rank of a sum of two matrices is at most the sum of their individual ranks: rank(A+B) <= rank(A) + rank(B).
keywords.example: If rank(A)=1 and rank(B)=2, then rank(A+B) <= 3.

keywords.explain: rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)), not equal to rank(A)*rank(B). The product cannot have more independent rows than either factor.
keywords.example: If rank(A)=1 and rank(B)=2, rank(AB) <= 1, not 2.

understand.visual_alt: Four statements about rank and determinant; II and III hold, I and IV do not.

given.aim: Determine which of the four statements about rank(M) and det(M) for n x n real matrices A and B are true

terms.meaning: Number of linearly independent rows (or columns) of matrix M
terms.example: rank(I_n) = n for the identity matrix
terms.connects: Used in statements I and III

terms.meaning: Determinant of matrix M; scalar value encoding invertibility and scaling factor
terms.example: det(I_n) = 1, det(O) = 0 where O is zero matrix
terms.connects: Used in statements II and IV

terms.meaning: det(AB) = det(A) * det(B) for square matrices of the same size

given.plan: Verify each statement: I (rank product - counterexample), II (det product - theorem), III (rank sub-additivity - theorem), IV (det addition - counterexample).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: rank(AB) = rank(A) * rank(B)
steps.apply: Counterexample: Let A = B = [[1,0],[0,0]] (rank 1 each). AB = [[1,0],[0,0]] has rank 1. But rank(A)*rank(B) = 1*1 = 1. This case agrees, but try A = [[1,1],[1,1]] (rank 1) and B = [[1,-1],[-1,1]] (rank 1). AB = [[0,0],[0,0]] has rank 0, but 1*1 = 1. So I is FALSE.
steps.note: rank(AB) can be strictly less than rank(A)*rank(B); the correct bound is rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: det(AB) = det(A) * det(B)
steps.apply: This is the multiplicative property of determinants, a standard theorem in linear algebra. It holds for all n x n square matrices over any field.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III: rank(A+B) <= rank(A) + rank(B)
steps.apply: This follows because the column space (or row space) of A+B is contained in the span of the column spaces of A and B. The dimension of a sum of subspaces is at most the sum of their dimensions.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement IV: det(A+B) <= det(A) + det(B)
steps.formula_raw: det is NOT sub-additive in general
steps.apply: Counterexample: A = B = [[1,0],[0,1]] (det = 1 each). A+B = [[2,0],[0,2]], det(A+B) = 4. But det(A)+det(B) = 1+1 = 2. Here 4 > 2, so IV is FALSE.
steps.note: Determinant has no general additive inequality.

solution.result: Statements II and III are TRUE. Answer: C. II and III only.

questions.formula_note: det(AB) = det(A)*det(B) is the multiplicative property of determinants; rank sub-additivity rank(A+B) <= rank(A)+rank(B) follows from subspace dimension arguments.
